skill.name: azure-reliability
skill.description: Assess Azure App Service and Azure Functions reliability across zone redundancy, storage redundancy, health probes, and multi-region failover. Produces a checklist and guides confirmed CLI or IaC remediation.
skill.summary: Assess and improve reliability for Azure App Service and Azure Functions.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found these are mostly Markdown examples for Azure CLI, Bicep, Terraform, and service documentation. No prompt injection, credential theft, obfuscation, or malicious exfiltration was found; the real risk is that the skill can guide live Azure changes and repository IaC edits after user consent.

medium_findings.title: Live Azure Resource Modification Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for operational risk. The skill instructs agents to run Azure CLI commands against live resources and can create secondary resource groups, storage accounts, plans, and apps after confirmation. This is legitimate DevOps behavior, but misuse could change production infrastructure or cost.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe running Azure CLI operations and creating cloud resources. The workflow includes consent gates, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Infrastructure-as-Code Patch and Deploy Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for change-management risk. The skill directs agents to find Bicep or Terraform files, patch reliability settings, and run deployments after confirmation. This can be appropriate, but it can also modify repositories and deployed infrastructure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows direct file discovery, IaC patching, and deployment guidance. Explicit confirmation steps lower the security concern but do not remove operational impact.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious command execution. The many shell and Azure CLI detections are Markdown command examples for assessment and remediation, not hidden executable code inside the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are inside fenced Markdown examples and align with the skill purpose. They are still relevant to operational risk when an agent chooses to execute them.

low_findings.title: PowerShell and Filesystem Patterns Are Local Discovery Instructions
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for path traversal or malicious filesystem access. The evidence shows local IaC discovery and relative documentation links, not arbitrary file reads or writes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions target expected infrastructure folders and documentation references. No evidence of secret harvesting or arbitrary path traversal was found.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Function Detection Is a C# Attribute Example
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for dynamic code execution. The flagged Functions reference contains a C# health endpoint example using an Azure Functions attribute, not a dynamic function constructor.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited snippet is a static C# method with an Azure Functions attribute. It does not construct or evaluate code dynamically.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation or Probe Placeholders
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for network exfiltration. The URLs are Microsoft Learn references, Azure endpoint placeholders, or example azcopy targets used in documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs do not send secrets to third parties and are clearly documentation or user-owned Azure endpoint examples. No credential exfiltration pattern was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure CLI Commands Can Change Cloud State
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains examples that update storage redundancy and create multi-region resources. These are expected for this DevOps skill but require confirmation and least-privilege Azure access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly modify Azure resources. Context shows intended remediation, not stealth behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Storage Migration Guidance Has Data Availability Impact
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill guides storage redundancy migration and explicitly notes long-running operations. This is a legitimate reliability action with availability, cost, and deployment sequencing risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The source explicitly discusses migration duration, sequencing, and potential apply problems. These are operational hazards, not malware indicators.

dangerous_patterns.title: Deployment Commands Are User-Confirmed but High Impact
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs the agent to run azd, ARM, or Terraform deployments after explicit consent. Publishing is reasonable with warnings because the workflow is transparent and scoped.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show direct deployment execution. The repeated consent gates reduce likelihood of accidental execution but not blast radius.

content.value_statement: Azure PaaS apps can hide single-region and storage redundancy gaps. This skill checks App Service and Functions reliability, then guides confirmed remediation.
actual_capabilities: Discovers Azure App Service and Azure Functions resources in a selected scope.
Checks compute zone redundancy, storage redundancy, health probes, and multi-region failover.
Builds a feature-pivoted reliability checklist with resource-level status notes.
Guides Azure CLI remediation for supported App Service and Functions scenarios.
Patches Bicep or Terraform examples for persistent reliability configuration.
Separates quick fixes from storage migrations and multi-region deployments.
limitations: The shipped service deep dives cover App Service and Azure Functions only.
It requires Azure authentication and adequate permissions for live assessment or changes.
Storage migrations and multi-region deployments can take time and increase cost.
It does not replace architecture review, load testing, or incident response planning.

use_cases.title: Assess production reliability
use_cases.description: Review App Service and Functions resources for single-region, storage, health probe, and zone redundancy gaps.

use_cases.title: Patch cloud infrastructure safely
use_cases.description: Update Bicep or Terraform definitions so reliability settings persist after deployment.

use_cases.title: Plan disaster recovery improvements
use_cases.description: Identify which reliability upgrades are quick wins and which require migration planning or higher spend.

prompt_templates.title: Check one app
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess the reliability posture of my Azure Function App named <app-name> in resource group <resource-group>.

prompt_templates.title: Review a resource group
prompt_templates.prompt: Check App Service and Azure Functions reliability in resource group <resource-group>. Show zone redundancy, storage redundancy, health probes, and failover status.

prompt_templates.title: Create a fix plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a remediation plan for the red reliability findings. Separate quick CLI fixes from storage migration and multi-region work.

prompt_templates.title: Patch infrastructure as code
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect my infra folder and patch Bicep or Terraform so Azure App Service and Functions reliability settings persist across deploys.

output_examples.input: Assess my Function App reliability in rg-prod.
output_examples.output: A four-row reliability checklist showing compute zone redundancy, storage redundancy, health probes, and multi-region failover, with affected resources listed.

output_examples.input: Fix the red items using IaC.
output_examples.output: A staged remediation plan that patches supported infrastructure files, asks before deployment, and separates storage migration from quick changes.

output_examples.input: Set up multi-region failover for my app.
output_examples.output: A consent-gated plan for secondary region resources, global routing, health probes, DNS, cost impact, and final verification.

best_practices: Run assessment before remediation so every change maps to a specific reliability gap.
Prefer IaC patches when using azd or Terraform so live fixes are not overwritten.
Confirm cost, downtime, and migration impact before storage or multi-region changes.
anti_patterns: Do not run live Azure changes without explicit user confirmation.
Do not bundle storage migration with quick compute or health probe changes.
Do not apply unsupported service fixes outside App Service and Azure Functions.

faq.question: Which Azure services does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports Azure App Service and Azure Functions. Container Apps are noted as planned but not deeply assessed.

faq.question: Can it change live Azure resources?
faq.answer: Yes, if the user selects the CLI path and confirms each change. This requires Azure permissions.

faq.question: Can it update Terraform or Bicep?
faq.answer: Yes. It can inspect infrastructure files and guide patches for supported reliability settings.

faq.question: Does it handle storage migration automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide migration steps after explicit consent. Storage migration can take hours or days.

faq.question: Does it assign a reliability score?
faq.answer: No. It uses feature status rows instead of numeric scores or grades.

faq.question: What permissions are needed?
faq.answer: Reader access is enough for assessment. Contributor access is needed for configuration changes.
